data.title: New York police commissioner credited with crime drop to stay on under Mamdani
data.description:
Key points:
  • Mamdani says he admires Tisch's work on police corruption, driving down crime
  • Tisch says she and Mamdani share many of same public safety goals
  • Mamdani pledges to retain current number of police officers, create Department of Public Safety

By Jonathan Allen and Maria Tsvetkova

Jessica Tisch, the New York City police commissioner who has overseen a drop in violent crime under outgoing Mayor Eric Adams, has agreed to remain in the post under newly elected Zohran Mamdani, who recently apologized for his past criticisms of the department.

Although Mamdani said during his campaign that he would like to retain Tisch, it was uncertain until their joint announcement on Wednesday whether Tisch would agree to serve under a mayor who has expounded progressive critiques on policing and the criminal justice system.

Earlier this year, Mamdani, a 34-year-old Democratic state lawmaker and democratic socialist, publicly apologized to the city's police officers for his past public comments in which he described some of the department's officers as racist and a threat to public safety. Even so, there were concerns among some New York City voters and within the New York City Police Department that Mamdani would be soft on crime while frustrating the police in fighting it.

On Wednesday, Mamdani and Tisch announced that they were in agreement on fundamental issues and would prioritize continuity for the city of more than 8 million people, the country's biggest, where murders and shootings have dropped sharply under Tisch's first year in the job.

"I have admired her work cracking down on corruption in the upper echelons of the police department, driving down crime in New York City, and standing up for New Yorkers in the face of authoritarianism," Mamdani, who was elected on November 4, said in a statement.

Tisch, 44, was appointed police commissioner in November 2024 by Adams, the fourth person to oversee the nation's largest police department in an 18-month span, as Adams and members of his inner circle faced criminal investigations into allegations of corruption.

Tisch said in a statement that she and Mamdani "share many of the same public safety goals for New York City: lowering crime, making communities safer, rooting out corruption, and giving our officers the tools, support, and resources they need to carry out their noble work."

The mayor and his police commissioner come from contrasting backgrounds. Mamdani will be the city's first Muslim mayor. Tisch is the first Jewish woman to lead the New York Police Department. Mamdani, the son of a college professor and a filmmaker, has been sharply critical of the city's wealthy elite, while the commissioner is an heir of the billionaire Tisch family, which made its fortune with the Loews Corp and owns a 50% stake in the National Football League's New York Giants franchise.

MAMDANI CALLED FOR POLICE DEFUNDING IN 2020

During his election campaign, Mamdani publicly apologized to police officers for his past criticisms, in particular for calling on social media in 2020 for the department to be defunded for being "racist, anti-queer & a major threat to public safety."

Disavowing his past calls for defunding, Mamdani pledged to maintain the current number of police officers after he is sworn in on January 1. He also pledged to create a new Department of Community Safety to deploy mental health experts and social workers to some calls currently handled by police officers.

U.S. President Donald Trump, a Republican and a native New Yorker who falsely denigrated Mamdani as a communist, has said he would try to punish New Yorkers if they voted for him, issuing legally shaky threats to withhold federal funds. But this week, Trump said he would consider meeting with Mamdani.

Adams, who abandoned his bid for a second term as mayor as his popularity plummeted this year, commended Mamdani, whom he had roundly criticized in the past.

Mamdani, in retaining Tisch, Adams said in a statement, "is recognizing our public-safety efforts were right and that they will continue into the future."

data.title: ESALQ Poultry Analysis
data.description: poultry analysis POULTRY/CEPEA: Chicken meat prices drop; competitiveness increases against competing meats Cepea, 19 - Chicken meat has been competitive again against other products (beef and pork meat). This scenario is related to price drops of chicken meat this month, to increases of beef quotations and to the stability of pork meat values. It is worth noting that price rises of chicken meat in October reduced the competitiveness of this product against competing meats. In November (up to Nov. 18), values of the chilled chicken meat in the wholesale market of the Greater São Paulo averaged BRL 7.81/kg, for a decrease of 2.2% against that in October. Besides the demand decrease in the second fortnight of the month, players surveyed by Cepea say that the recent price dropis also linked to the high supply of chicken to slaughter. In spite of that, sales are moving at a good pace. Beef carcass quotations in the wholesale market of the Greater SP averaged BRL 22.53/kg in November, 4.1% above that verified in the month before. The good pace of exports has keeping values firm. Chilled chicken meat prices were 14.72 Reais per kilo lower than beef carcass values in November, a gap that is 7.8% higher than in October. Special pork carcass values are stable from October to the partial of this month, averaging BRL 12.54/kg in the wholesale market of SP. Quotations of the chilled chicken meat in the wholesale market of the Greater São Paulo were 4.73 Reais per kilo below values of pork meat in November, a gap that is 4% higher than that observed in October. For the Portuguese version please refer to or click on <ESQ/POULT/ANALYS>

data.title: End to Fed's Balance-Sheet Runoff Followed Staff Guidance — Market Talk
data.description: Roberto Perli, manager of the Fed's presence in financial markets, told policymakers at the Fed's October meeting that financial conditions suggested the Fed should end its balance-sheet runoff soon, according to the minutes of the meeting. Indeed, the Fed followed through and voted to end balance-sheet runoff as of Dec. 1. Perli noted that financial pressures in short-term lending markets had risen. He noted that if pressures continued to worsen, excessive money-market volatility could impede the Fed's ability to fine-tune interest rates in accordance with its monetary-policy decisions. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

data.title: Mosaic ImmunoEngineering Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Mosaic ImmunoEngineering Inc., a company focused on advancing and commercializing cancer therapies, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter of 2025. The report highlights the company's financial performance and operational activities, reflecting both improvements and ongoing challenges in its business operations.

Financial Highlights

Net Loss: Mosaic ImmunoEngineering reported a net loss of $(165,361) for the three months ended September 30, 2025, compared to $(186,730) for the same period in 2024, indicating a reduction in losses. For the nine months ended September 30, 2025, the net loss was $(518,709), compared to $(768,939) for the same period in 2024, reflecting improved financial performance.

Basic and Diluted Loss Per Common Share: The basic and diluted loss per common share was $(0.02) for the three months ended September 30, 2025, compared to $(0.03) for the same period in 2024. For the nine months ended September 30, 2025, the loss per share was $(0.07), compared to $(0.11) for the same period in 2024.

Loss from Operations: The company reported a loss from operations of $(144,358) for the three months ended September 30, 2025, compared to $(186,430) for the same period in 2024, showing a decrease in operating losses. For the nine months ended September 30, 2025, the loss from operations was $(467,986), compared to $(729,498) for the same period in 2024, indicating a reduction in operating expenses.

Total Operating Expenses: Total operating expenses were $144,358 for the three months ended September 30, 2025, compared to $186,430 for the same period in 2024, primarily due to decreased payroll and related costs. For the nine months ended September 30, 2025, total operating expenses were $467,986, compared to $729,498 for the same period in 2024, reflecting cost reduction efforts.

Business Highlights

Research and Development Focus: Mosaic ImmunoEngineering is primarily focused on advancing and commercializing cancer therapies. The company has historically worked on early-stage product candidates and is actively pursuing new product candidates and platforms to expand its portfolio.

Operational Challenges: The company is facing significant operational challenges due to its limited cash reserves and the need to raise additional capital to fund future operations. This has impacted their ability to conduct business operations effectively.

License Agreements: Mosaic has several license agreements, including those with UC San Diego, which involve the development and commercialization of technology related to plant virus protein nanoparticles and novel vaccine candidates. However, the company has terminated some agreements based on strategic evaluations.

Future Outlook and Strategic Plans: The company is exploring strategic partnerships and acquisitions to establish a new product pipeline. This includes a potential acquisition of rights to Oncotelic’s clinical stage necroptosis cancer therapies and access to AI technologies for identifying immunotherapy combinations, although the binding term sheet for this expired on June 30, 2025.

Operational Cost Management: The company has been managing its operational costs by reducing payroll and related expenses due to a reduced time commitment by certain employees. This has been a significant factor in the decrease in both research and development and general administrative expenses.

Consulting and Related Services: Mosaic has entered into a Master Services Agreement with Oncotelic to perform advisory and related services, which contributed to other income during the reporting period.

Going Concern and Management’s Plans: The company has expressed substantial doubt about its ability to continue as a going concern due to its current financial position and the uncertainties associated with raising additional capital.

SEC Filing: Mosaic ImmunoEngineering Inc. [ CPMV ] - 10-Q - Nov. 19, 2025

data.title: Gold Holds Gains After Minutes
data.description:
Gold held above the $4,070 mark on Wednesday after the Fed’s October minutes revealed a clear split among officials with most seeing further cuts as likely at some point but many signalling that a December move is not a foregone conclusion, a nuance that trimmed near term rate cut odds and removed some impetus from bullion.

That cautious tilt left gold caught between a firmer dollar and lingering safe haven and geopolitical demand, so prices held near recent levels rather than extending a rally.

Markets have pared expectations for a December cut to roughly the 30s percent on the CME FedWatch tool which has refocused attention on the delayed US jobs report and further Fed messaging as the next likely triggers for direction.

The jobs print is expected to show only modest payroll gains around 50,000 Meanwhile, concerns over elevated tech valuations have weighed on risk sentiment, further bolstering gold’s appeal as a safe-haven amid ongoing equity market weakness.

data.title: South Carolina Authority to Sell $48.7 Million of Economic Development Revenue Bonds
data.description:
By Chris Wack

The South Carolina Jobs-Economic Development Authority plans to sell $48.7 million of economic development revenue bonds for a retirement community in Charleston, the state's biggest city.

Proceeds from the Series 2025 bonds are expected to be loaned to Bishop Gadsden Episcopal Retirement Community to pay a portion of the costs of buying, constructing and equipping additions and improvements to the community, according to a document posted Tuesday on MuniOS.

Bishop Gadsden Episcopal Retirement Community is a South Carolina non-profit corporation that owns and operates a continuing care retirement community. The Charleston community currently consists of a 79-unit assisted living facility, a 132-bed skilled nursing facility, and 283 independent living units with related common areas.

Interest on the bonds will be payable starting on April 1, and then the following Oct. 1. Interest rates and yields have not yet been set. The bonds accumulate principle from 2029 through 2056.

The bonds are limited obligations of the authority.

The South Carolina Jobs-Economic Development Authority is a public body corporate and politic and an agency of the state, to buy, by construction or purchase, properties to promote and develop the business and economic welfare of the state.

Fitch assigned the bonds a rating of BBB-.

Ziegler is the lead underwriter.

Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com

data.title: Silver may be the 'poor man's gold,' but its 74% surge this year looks far from over
data.description:
By Myra P. Saefong

The precious metal may represent one of the few cases where cheaper is better - not just when it comes to cost, but in terms of quality for investors

Silver's up 74% this year, far outpacing the roughly 55% gain for gold prices so far in 2025.

Silver may represent one of the few cases where cheaper is better - not just when it comes to cost, but in terms of quality for investors.

The asset known as "poor man's gold" is the top performer in the precious-metals space so far this year, and it may have plenty of room to run even higher.

"As the cheaper, 'poor man's gold,' silver has always been the monetary option of the masses, and in fact has historically circulated more as money, while gold has functioned more as a store of value," said Brien Lundin, editor of Gold Newsletter. Silver has always been cheaper than gold, yet still highly valued, he noted.

This is natural, since the smaller denominations of silver currencies allowed for more efficient usage, Lundin told MarketWatch. So, silver coins were more suited for purchasing bread and other sundries, while gold was more suited for buying a home.

Now, silver (SI00) is the top performer among major precious metals, with prices up 74% year to date and recently climbing to a fresh nominal record high. That compares to a nearly 55% rise in gold (GC00), 70% climb in platinum (PL00) and a nearly 54% rise in palladium (PA00) futures so far this year, as of late Wednesday morning, according to a Dow Jones Market Data analysis of FactSet figures.

That says a lot given that much of the attention has fallen upon gold, which climbed to a record-high close of $4,359.40 on Oct. 20. But silver marked a record-high finish too - at $53.46 on Nov. 12 - and it's poised to mark its best year since 2010.

"When gold takes off to the upside, many investors shift toward silver because they can get so many more ounces for the same dollar amount," said Stefan Gleason, president and chief executive officer at Money Metals Exchange.

Silver joins nation's critical-minerals list

But it's not just quantity when it comes to silver - it's also quality.

Silver has both monetary and industrial features, and each of its "personalities is highly charming," said Gleason.

"Its monetary qualities are time-tested for millenia, and in many languages, the word for silver literally means money." For example, the Spanish word "plata" can be used to refer to either money or silver.

And on the industrial end, silver's uses are "exploding," thanks to its status as the "best reflector of visible light, the most conductive metal for electricity and head, and its remarkable effectiveness as a natural biocide," Gleason noted. The U.S. Interior Department recently added silver to the the nation's critical-minerals list.

Silver is "one of the most useful metals on earth" and it's historically inexpensive relative to gold, so that makes it a "compelling value play," Gleason said.

Inflation-adjusted value

A key difference between the two metals, however, is that while gold earlier this year topped its inflation-adjusted record high from the 1980s, silver has a long way to go to reach its own inflation-adjusted record high of $209.71 from January 1980, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Silver remains far below its own historical high, noted Will Rhind, CEO of GraniteShares. That gap is "meaningful," as silver has often outperformed gold during strong bull cycles, he said. "Its current discount implies substantial catch-up potential if the broader precious-metals trend continues."

In Wednesday dealings, the most active December futures contract for silver traded at $50.71 an ounce on Comex - down $159 or almost 76% from that inflation-adjusted record high.

Still, silver is positioned to remain among the leaders in the precious-metal markets as long as current drivers persist, said Rhind. "Supply-demand fundamentals remain tight, and investor appetite for hard assets continue to be strong," he said. And while volatility is "inherent," with double-digit pullbacks normal, the broader trend remains "constructive."

Inflation is still elevated and long-term confidence in fiat currencies are under pressure, so silver's risk-reward profile is "compelling," said Rhind.

Meanwhile, silver's strength has been driven by "both sides of its identity: industrial metal and monetary metal," he added. That "dual role" allows silver to respond to both manufacturing trends and safe-haven buying, giving it "more torque when both forces align," said Rhind.

The metal's physical market is also on track for another annual deficit in 2025, its fifth in a row, according to the Silver Institute.

Silver has been "undervalued for decades," Paul Mladjenovic, author of "Investing in Gold and Silver for Dummies," told MarketWatch.

The demand side has increased due to artificial-intelligence-related technology, and smartphone and energy-related needs are still strong, he said, leading to several years of supply shortages. Mladjenovic expects those shortages to continue into 2026 until new exploration yields more supply.

Looking ahead, Mladjenovic expects silver to climb to $75 an ounce by the second half of next year. That would mark a rise of more than 30% from current prices.

-Myra P. Saefong

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: AI set to redefine shopping and payments, Affirm CEO says
data.description:
Key points:
  • Agentic AI to eliminate predatory business models, Levchin says
  • BNPL market grew 9.9% in 2024, Adobe Analytics reports
  • Composable solutions key for retail success in AI era, Levchin argues

By Hannah Lang

Max Levchin, CEO and co-founder of Affirm, which offers buy now, pay later plans, said artificial intelligence is poised to transform shopping by taking over human tasks and making financial products more transparent.

AI agents will soon be able to instantly weed out products with prohibitively high interest rates or late fees, forcing businesses to rethink fee structures, Levchin said at the Reuters Momentum AI Finance conference in New York on Tuesday.

"I think we're headed for a future where we have a profound elimination, for lack of a better term, of business models that are designed to prey on stupidity, or lack of attention ... because it's all going to get done by the bots," he said.

Agentic AI needs minimal human intervention to make decisions and is widely considered the next iteration of generative AI. AI agents will soon shop and pay on behalf of consumers, and recommend the best financial products to accommodate their purchases, Levchin predicted.

"My dumb, young, naive self in college signing up for a deferred interest credit card would have never known to read the fine print," said Levchin, a PayPal co-founder.

"But even if I did, I would have been lost in the morass within seconds because it's deliberately obscure. AI does not have a problem with that. It can read the finest of prints, it will find the 'gotcha.'"

Buy now, pay later (BNPL) providers like Affirm and Klarna offer loans that shoppers repay in installments spread over as many as 36 months, although the most common plan has four payments.

BNPL exploded in popularity as the COVID-19 pandemic forced more shoppers online and has grown rapidly, driving $82.4 billion in online spending in 2024, up 9.9% from 2023, according to Adobe Analytics.

Levchin outlined Affirm's approach to AI in an August letter to shareholders, arguing that the company was "built for this," with products that can be integrated into chatbots, internet browsers and digital wallets.

The shift toward agentic shopping will create winners and losers across retail and payments, with companies that embrace composable, adaptive solutions positioned to thrive, he added.

Among other large companies pivoting towards autonomous AI, Walmart unveiled plans in July to roll out a suite of AI-powered "super agents" to help customers and streamline operations. The retailer is betting on AI to drive its e-commerce growth, aiming for online sales to account for 50% of its total sales within five years, Walmart has said.

data.title: Warner Music Group, Udio Settle AI Copyright Suit, to Launch Joint Platform
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Warner Music Group is settling its copyright-infringement lawsuit against artificial-intelligence music generator Udio, with the two companies agreeing to jointly launch a licensed music creation service.

WMG, Sony Music and Universal Music Group sued Udio in June 2024, alleging that Udio's AI-generated music violated their copyrights. Under Udio's agreement, its models will train on licensed and authorized music from WMG, one of the largest music labels.

"We're unwaveringly committed to the protection of the rights of our artists and songwriters, and Udio has taken meaningful steps to ensure that the music on its service will be authorized and licensed," WMG Chief Executive Officer Robert Kyncl said.

The companies said artists signed to WMG can opt in to the planned, subscription-based service, adding that they would be credited and paid for their participation. Udio would also expand its measures to protect the rights of artists and songwriters.

The financial terms of the settlement weren't disclosed. The joint service is expected to launch in 2026. Until then, Udio said users would continue to have access to the company's current platform to smooth the transition.

Udio CEO Andrew Sanchez said the deal "is a crucial step towards realizing a future in which technology amplifies creativity and unlocks new opportunities for artists and songwriters."

AI music platforms have been the subject of controversy and legal scrutiny in the music industry. Record labels are navigating how to embrace AI while protecting copyrighted work, The Wall Street Journal has reported. Universal Music Group and Udio signed a similar agreement last month.

Suno, another AI music generator which has raised a $2.45 billion dollar valuation, remains in litigation with the three record labels, according to the Journal.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: Eversource shares slump after Connecticut regulator blocks $2.4 billion Aquarion sale
data.description:
Eversource Energy shares fell over 10% on Wednesday after Connecticut regulators rejected the company's proposal to sell its water utility business, dealing a setback to the utility's efforts to streamline operations and reduce debt.

Eversource agreed in January to sell Aquarion in a deal valued at $2.4 billion, after acquiring the water utility in 2017 for about $1.7 billion.

The regulator concluded that the deal fails managerial suitability and responsibility requirements "in a manner consistent with the public interest", despite meeting financial and technological criteria, along with likely ensuring continued safe and reliable service to customers.

Eversource responded that the state was interested in expanding a non-profit model. However, when this was tested, Connecticut Public Utilities Regulatory Authority (PURA) found it challenging to transition from the investor-owned model.

The 2024 special act directed the state to explore if a non-profit ownership model could better serve water customers.

Citi analyst Ryan Levine said, "Given this decision, the company's cash flow and balance sheet improvement path remains challenging."

He added that the new PURA is not as constructive as the market hoped.

The application for the transfer of control of Aquarion was filed by Eversource, Aquarion Water Authority (AWA) and the South Central Connecticut Regional Water Authority (RWA).

Aquarion provides public water supply and distribution services to nearly 226,000 customers in 73 cities and towns across Connecticut, Massachusetts and New Hampshire.

The deal was expected to close in late 2025.

data.title: The Marzetti Co. Raises Dividend 5% to $1/Shr
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

The Marzetti Co. raised its quarterly cash dividend 5.2% to $1 a share.

The increase from 95 cents marks the food producer's 63rd consecutive year of increasing its dividend.

The new payout, equal to $4 a year, represents an annual yield of 2.4% based on Tuesday's closing price of $170.21.

The dividend will be payable on Dec. 31 to shareholders of record Dec. 5.

Shareholders re-elected four incumbent directors to the board during the annual meeting, Marzetti said Wednesday.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Einride Sues Maersk Over U.S. Rollout of Battery-Electric Big Rigs — WSJ
data.description:
By Paul Berger

Swedish battery-electric truck startup Einride is suing Danish logistics giant Maersk over an aborted deal to deploy hundreds of zero-emission trucks in the U.S.

The companies in 2022 touted the rollout of the 300 Einride trucks as Maersk's "largest heavy-duty electric truck deployment to date" and "the first large-scale use of Einride's digital road freight operating system."

Einride, in heavily redacted court papers filed in Los Angeles County Superior Court, says A.P. Moller-Maersk and its California-based affiliates reneged on the deal "after not being able to live up to their own sales targets for electric capacity."

A Maersk representative said the Copenhagen-based logistics company "had no choice but to terminate our contract with Einride in November 2024 due to their failure to deliver additional electric vehicles already ordered and also their failure to pay their vendors at the time."

The representative declined further comment citing the ongoing litigation.

Einride was founded in 2016 and recently said it intends to go public next year. A representative for the company said: "We don't agree with Maersk's characterization of the circumstances and that is partly why we initiated legal action against Maersk."

The lawsuit, filed in November 2024, was reported earlier Wednesday by Danish business newspaper Børsen.

The court battle is one more sign of the struggles logistics companies face as they try to hit sustainability targets by deploying zero-emission heavy-duty trucks in the U.S.

Efforts to boost demand for battery-electric big rigs in America were set back this year after the Trump administration blocked or rolled back regulations that pushed logistics operators toward cleaner technologies.

Battery-electric trucks are more expensive than diesel big rigs because of high upfront costs for the vehicles and charging infrastructure. Proponents of the technology say that, compared with diesel trucks, the costs for battery-electric equipment even out over the life cycle of the vehicle because of lower bills for repairs and energy.

Still, carriers charge a premium for low- and zero-emission transportation. Logistics companies say many shippers are reluctant to pay extra for green services.

Maersk revealed in 2021 that it had invested in Einride through its venture capital arm Maersk Growth. The companies teamed up in 2022 with a deal to deploy 300 trucks and 150 charging stations between 2023 and 2025 in California, Illinois and New Jersey.

Einride, in its lawsuit, says it placed orders for heavy-duty electric trucks and charging infrastructure and started recruiting workers across the U.S. based on Maersk's commitment. But in the fall of 2024, before all of the trucks and chargers had been deployed, Maersk began to reconsider its U.S. electric operations.

In an Oct. 16, 2024, email to Einride, cited in the lawsuit, Maersk said it couldn't "see a way forward where we can both run a sustainable business as partners under the current construct — considering external market conditions and given the current situation." Einride said in the suit that at a meeting in Copenhagen in early November that year, Maersk threatened to pull out of the agreements unless Einride could provide "hefty discounts."

Einride is seeking several remedies including damages and payment of owed fees.

Write to Paul Berger at paul.berger@wsj.com

data.title: Uncertainty About Fed Cuts Looms Large for Munis — Market Talk
data.description: The path of interest rates could be one of the biggest hurdles for municipal bond investors in 2026. Aberdeen's Miguel Laranjeiro points to opposite forces that could make the Fed cut rates faster--or not. He says economic growth is likely to benefit from tax cuts and the FIFA Soccer World Cup, among other things, making the central bank more reluctant to cut. On the other hand, the Fed will have new leadership and chances are the body ends up leaning more towards monetary easing. "There's definitely room for both sides," Laranjeiro says, adding that a dovish Fed is his more likely scenario. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Fed Split on Need for Rate Cuts: Minutes
data.description:
Many Federal Reserve officials favored lowering the target range for the federal funds rate in October, though some could have supported leaving rates unchanged, and several opposed a reduction, minutes from the last FOMC meeting showed.

When discussing the near-term path of monetary policy, participants expressed sharply divergent views on what decision would be most appropriate at the December meeting.

Most believed further downward adjustments to the target range would likely be warranted, though several of them emphasized that another 25bps cut might not be appropriate in December.

A number of participants judged that an additional rate cut in December could be suitable if the economy evolved broadly as expected over the intermeeting period.

Many others suggested that, under their outlooks, keeping the target range unchanged for the remainder of the year would likely be appropriate.

The Fed lowered the federal funds rate by 25 bps to 3.75%–4.00% at its October 2025 meeting.
